levels.body: The title is a reference to the Second Coming in the Christian religion: “[the] Second Coming of Christ. The prophesied return of Christ to earth at the Last Judgment.” (“Second Coming, *n.*”, Sense 1). Furthermore, it is related to “[t]he events described in the revelation of St John; the Second Coming of Christ and the ultimate destruction of the world” (“Apocalypse, *n.*”, Sense 3.a.). It is also understood as a disaster resulting in drastic, irreversible damage “to human society or the environment” (“Apocalypse, *n.*”, Sense 3.b.).
worksCited: “Apocalypse, *n.*” *Oxford English Dictionary*, Oxford UP, June 2025, doi.org/10.1093/OED/2355444502. Accessed 10 June 2025.
“Second Coming, *n.*” *Oxford English Dictionary*, Oxford UP, July 2023, doi.org/10.1093/OED/1061497653. Accessed 10 June 2025

levels.body: In the first draft of the poem, there is no ‘second coming’ as Yeats originally used “second birth” as the poem’s working title (Yeats 151–57).
worksCited: Yeats, W. B. *Michael Robartes and the Dancer: Manuscript Materials*. Edited by Thomas Francis Parkinson and Anne Brannen, Cornell University Press, 1994.

levels.body: The Second Coming has its origin in the Christian Bible, more precisely the Book of Revelation. In Christianity, the Book of Revelation mentions the Second Coming of Jesus Christ and the apocalypse that takes place: “[...] his wrath is come [...]” (*King James Version*, Revelation 6:17), “[...] for the time is come for thee to reap [...]” (*King James Version*, Revelation 14:15).
worksCited: *The Bible*, King James, Version, HarperCollins Christian Publishing, Inc., 1979.

levels.body:
The world that was once known is coming to a violent end; as seen in the line 3 “Things fall apart”, line 4 “Mere anarchy is loosed upon the world”, line 6 “The ceremony of innocence is drowned”. The world order is collapsing in every aspect, be it moral, political or cultural. However, with the end of the world, there is also a new beginning; a new era that will be born. This provides a hopeful note in the poem of a possible better new age that will come. The lines 9 and 10 of the poem point to something emerging from this destruction “Surely some revelation is at hand;/ Surely the Second Coming is at hand”. The new era is most likely to be one of irrational force, represented by “A shape with lion body and the head of a man, / A gaze blank and pitiless as the sun” (Lines 14,15). The shape is going to be born as told in the last two lines of the poem 21–22 “And what rough beast, its hour come round at last / Slouches towards Bethlehem to be born?”. In Christianity, Jesus Christ was born in Bethlehem, and now the beast will be born there too, reflecting Yeat’s idea of a new age coming- a second birth. This rough beast indicates the entering of the new era; however, it is left unsaid what the said beast will bring, having potential for both a better and worse era to come.

Bethlehem is the birthplace of Jesus Christ in Christianity, mentioned in the poem in the line 22 “Slouches towards Bethlehem to be born?” (Yeats 187).
worksCited: Yeats, W. B., and Richard J. Finneran. *The Collected Poems of W. B. Yeats Revised Second Edition*. New York: Simon and Schuster, 1996.

levels.body:
The Second Coming is related to Judgment Day, when all souls will be judged by God. The Book of Revelation deals with the end of times before the return of Jesus Christ, describing the different stages of the end of the world. The Second Coming of Jesus Christ will happen as a way to set the world right, as the first coming of Jesus was a result of the world’s wrongdoings and the need for riddance of sin. This Second Coming of Jesus Christ and the apocalypse are closely tied together, since the second coming is both a reward for the faithful and a punishment for sinners, though it also brings a new creation, a new birth.

In accordance with the Christian idea that the second coming is a reward for the faithful and punishment for the unfaithful, the fact that humanity is enduring the apocalyptic event presents humanity as unfaithful and deserving of punishment for their sins. If humanity was still faithful, they wouldn’t be living through the Second Coming which is actually their punishment. The Second Coming is a consequence of the world’s degeneration. This relates to the poem’s idea that one era is coming to an end and a new one being born out of its ruins.

“Second birth” combined with the mention of Bethlehem in line 22 evokes the association with the virgin birth which was later reused in the poem “‘Leda and the Swan”’, in which there is mention of sexual violation by Zeus in the shape of a swan (Deane 179). Looking at the “‘rough beast”’ in line 21 reinforces the understanding of the change from birth to coming. The beast is not yet born, it is still in the process of coming to the point of birth after a violation of the order.
worksCited: Deane, Seamus. *Strange Country: Modernity and Nationhood in Irish Writing since 1790*. Oxford University Press, 2006, p. 178-79.

levels.body: While the Second Coming is regarded as a reward and a punishment for the faithful, Yeats uses apocalyptic imagery “blood-dimmed tide” (line 5), “anarchy loosed upon the world” (line 4), “rough beast” (line 21) to represent the Second Coming as the end of the world, neither punishment nor reward. Humanity has turned away from God and did not follow his teachings.

The Second Coming further represents the end of an era of a civilization. In his work *A Vision*, Yeats explains how he sees the beginnings and endings of civilisations, represented by two gyres (cf [[widening-gyre]]). Through the gyres, Yeats explains his understanding of the world and history. The beginning of an era of history is represented by the beginning point of the gyre, and as time passes the gyre spins further away from the starting point. Half way through the first gyre, a new gyre begins. As the first gyre comes to an end, the second one rises and develops further. The second coming itself represents the second rising gyre. The poem addresses the end of one era, represented by the end stages of one gyre. 

Furthermore, in his work *A Vision*, Yeats writes about the end of the Christian era after 2000 years and the rise of a new religion. Following his philosophy and view on history from A Vision, it is conclusive that the new religion will be the opposite to the Christian religion, the next “antithetical religion” (Mann 15).
worksCited: Hühn, Peter. “14. W. B. Yeats: ‘The second coming.’” *The Narratological Analysis of Lyric Poetry*, 31 Dec. 2005, pp. 177–186, doi.org/10.1515/9783110897623.177. Accessed 18 June 2025.
Mann, Neil, et al. *W.B. Yeats’s A Vision: Explications and Contexts*. Clemson University Digital Press, 2012. Yeats, W. B. *Collected Poems*. Macmillan, 1950.

levels.body: On a different note, Yeats lived during and after World War I and wrote the poem shortly after the end of WWI. In the then contemporary context, the Second Coming can be applied to the context of the First World War, with the apocalyptic images, violence and state of anarchy. Line 4 of the poem “mere anarchy is loosed upon the world”, line 5 “the blood-dimmed tide is loosed” and line 6 “the ceremony of innocence is drowned” all evoke war images of violence and battle (Yeats 187).
worksCited: Yeats, W. B., and Richard J. Finneran. *The Collected Poems of W. B. Yeats Revised Second Edition*. New York: Simon and Schuster, 1996.
